figures.caption: Table 3: Workflow evaluation prompt styles. †Randomized STL parameters (mirror_y, scale_xy, scale_z, threshold); validated within ±0.05 absolute tolerance on each parameter value to account for LLM rounding. ‡STL parameters derived from optimization inputs.

Figure 6: Weighted RAG score contributions by prompt and LLM backend under RAG-on and Empty RAG conditions (3 runs each). RAG-off (all scores exactly 0) is omitted. RAG-on approaches 1.0 for most combinations; Empty RAG degrades substantially except for Gemini on P0, where the default volume fraction is likely memorized.

Figure 7: Average step completion rates for the cGAN HPC training benchmark. (a) Explicit: step-by-step tool instructions. (b) Natural: plain-language description. Each cell shows the mean fraction of runs completing that step, averaged across 10 seeds.

Figure 4: Combined overall score distributions for the FULL (a) and W-COND (b) prompt styles. FULL establishes baseline capabilities; W-COND reveals degradation for open-source models due to conditional reasoning demands.

Figure 5: Tool count vs. performance for the W-RAND style. The solid line shows the combined overall score (CO) declining with additional tool calls, while the dashed line shows the design quality score (DQ) remaining flat (≈0.5), indicating that extra calls penalize efficiency without improving engineering output.
Figure 9: Offline model quality metrics (COG, RVC, MMD, DPP) for agent-trained diffusion models vs. EngiBench baselines. Arrows indicate desired direction. Values averaged across available seeds.
Table 5: Workflow evaluation results for Beams2D (mean ± std). TC = Task Completion rate, CO = Combined Overall score. Bold = best model per metric per row.

Table 6: Workflow evaluation results for Photonics2D (mean ± std). TC = Task Completion rate, CO = Combined Overall score. Bold = best model per metric per row. Gray text shows Beams2D results on the same workflow styles for comparison.

Table 1: Representative selection of LLM-relevant engineering capabilities covered by ENGIAI versus prior work, grouped into Capabilities, Infrastructure, and Evaluation. ✓ = supported, × = not supported. Systems are split into engineering agent systems (top) and evaluation benchmarks (bottom).

Figure 1: Multi-agent architecture. From top to bottom: the user interface, the orchestration layer (supervisor agent routing prompts), the specialization layer (seven domain agents), and the execution layer (external services and tools).
Figure 8: Offline model quality metrics (COG, RVC, MMD, DPP) for agent-trained cGAN models vs. EngiBench baselines. Arrows indicate desired direction. Values averaged across available seeds.

Figure 3: Tool-calling heatmaps for the FULL (a) and W-COND (b) prompt styles. Each cell shows the average number of calls per tool across all samples. FULL shows consistent tool usage across models; W-COND reveals divergent patterns for the open-source models.
